content: Core Web Vitals thresholds: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) must be under 2.5 seconds (good), 2.5-4.0s (needs improvement), >4.0s (poor). First Input Delay (FID) replaced by Interaction to Next Paint (INP) must be under 200ms. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) must be under 0.1. These metrics directly affect Google search ranking since 2021.

Image optimization reduces page weight by 40-60%: Use WebP (25-34% smaller than JPEG) or AVIF (50% smaller than JPEG). Implement lazy loading with loading='lazy' for below-fold images. Use srcset for responsive images (320w, 640w, 1024w, 1920w). Serve images from CDN with auto-format negotiation. Target 100 KB max per hero image.

Next-gen image format comparison: JPEG averages 100 KB for a 1000x667 photo, WebP achieves the same quality at 66-75 KB, AVIF at 50-55 KB. Browser support (2024): WebP 97%+, AVIF 92%+. Use <picture> element with AVIF first, WebP fallback, JPEG last. Sharp (Node.js) or Pillow (Python) for server-side conversion.

Code splitting with dynamic imports reduces initial bundle size by 30-60%. Route-based splitting: React.lazy(() => import('./Page')) loads components only when navigated to. Component-based splitting for modals, charts, and editors that aren't immediately visible. Webpack SplitChunksPlugin extracts shared vendor code into a commons chunk.

Tree shaking eliminates dead code from ES module bundles. Requirements: use ES6 import/export (not CommonJS require), set sideEffects:false in package.json, and use production mode. Tree shaking can reduce bundle size by 20-40%. Libraries like lodash-es (ES module) tree-shake individual functions while lodash (CommonJS) imports the entire 70KB library.

CDN caching strategy: Set Cache-Control headers — static assets (JS/CSS/images): max-age=31536000 (1 year) with content-hashed filenames for cache busting. HTML pages: max-age=0, must-revalidate or s-maxage=60 for CDN caching with short TTL. API responses: private, no-cache or short max-age (60-300s). Stale-while-revalidate allows serving stale content while fetching fresh.

CDN performance impact: Reduces latency from 200-400ms (origin) to 10-50ms (edge). Global CDN providers (CloudFlare, Fastly, CloudFront) have 200-450+ POPs. Cache hit ratio should target 90%+ for static content. Edge computing (CloudFlare Workers, Lambda@Edge) runs logic at the edge with sub-millisecond cold start for personalization without origin roundtrips.

Database query optimization starts with EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE to reveal query plans. Key indicators: Sequential Scan on large tables (add index), Nested Loop with high row counts (consider hash join), Sort operations (add covering index). PostgreSQL: enable pg_stat_statements to find slow queries. MySQL: enable slow_query_log with long_query_time=0.5 seconds.

Index optimization rules: B-tree indexes for equality and range queries (90% of cases). Composite indexes follow leftmost prefix rule — index on (a,b,c) supports queries on (a), (a,b), (a,b,c) but NOT (b,c). Covering indexes include all SELECT columns, avoiding table lookups. Over-indexing slows writes by 10-30% per additional index. Target <5 indexes per table.

Denormalization trades storage for read performance: Store computed aggregates (order_total instead of SUM at query time), duplicate frequently joined data (user_name in orders table), and use materialized views for complex reports. Read-heavy systems (95%+ reads) benefit most. The trade-off is increased write complexity and potential data inconsistency.

N+1 query problem: Loading 100 orders then fetching each order's items individually generates 101 queries instead of 2. Solutions: Eager loading (SQL JOIN), Batch loading (WHERE id IN (...)), DataLoader pattern (batch + cache within request). In Django: select_related (JOIN) and prefetch_related (separate query). In SQLAlchemy: joinedload() and subqueryload().

DataLoader pattern batches and caches database lookups within a single request. Instead of N individual queries, DataLoader collects all keys in one tick and issues a single batch query. Facebook's original DataLoader processes up to 10,000 keys per batch. Implementation: collect keys → wait one event loop tick → batch fetch → distribute results.

Connection pooling reuses database connections instead of creating new ones per request. Creating a connection costs 20-100ms. Pool size formula: pool_size = (core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count, typically 10-20 for web apps. PgBouncer (external pooler) supports 10,000+ clients with 20 backend connections. Set pool_timeout=30s and max_overflow=10.

Multi-layer caching strategy: Browser cache (static assets, 1yr TTL) → CDN cache (HTML, 60s TTL) → Application cache (Redis/Memcached, computed results) → Database query cache (prepared statements, query plan cache). Each layer reduces latency: Browser 0ms, CDN 10-50ms, App cache 1-5ms, Database 5-50ms, Full query 50-500ms.

Redis caching patterns: Cache-aside (check cache, miss → query DB → write cache), Write-through (write to cache and DB simultaneously), Write-behind (write to cache, async batch to DB). TTL strategy: hot data 60-300s, warm data 1-24 hours, cold data 24-72 hours. Monitor cache hit ratio — target 95%+. Eviction policy: allkeys-lru for general caching.

Python profiling with py-spy: Sample-based profiler with <1% overhead, no code changes needed. Run: py-spy record -o profile.svg -- python app.py (generates flame graph). py-spy top shows real-time function call statistics. cProfile for deterministic profiling: python -m cProfile -s cumulative app.py. line_profiler for line-by-line timing of specific functions.

Chrome DevTools Performance panel: Record a page load or interaction, analyze the flame chart for long tasks (>50ms blocks the main thread). Lighthouse audit scores: Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, SEO (each 0-100, target 90+). Network tab waterfall identifies blocking resources. Coverage tab shows unused CSS/JS — typically 50-70% of loaded code is unused on first load.

Memory profiling in Python: tracemalloc (built-in) tracks memory allocations — tracemalloc.start() then take snapshots to compare. memory_profiler decorates functions with @profile for line-by-line memory usage. objgraph finds memory leaks by showing object reference counts. Common Python memory issues: large list comprehensions (use generators), circular references, and unclosed file handles.

Critical rendering path optimization: Minimize critical resources (inline critical CSS <14KB, defer non-critical JS). Reduce critical path length (minimize round trips — HTTP/2 multiplexing, preconnect to origins). Minimize critical bytes (compress with Brotli for 15-20% better compression than gzip). First Contentful Paint target: under 1.8 seconds on 4G mobile.

HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 performance gains: HTTP/2 multiplexes streams over a single TCP connection (eliminates head-of-line blocking at HTTP level). HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP) eliminating TCP head-of-line blocking entirely, with 0-RTT connection resumption. HTTP/3 reduces page load time by 10-15% on lossy networks. Enable with server config — no code changes needed.

Load testing with k6: Write JavaScript test scripts defining virtual users (VUs) and iterations. Ramp-up pattern: start with 10 VUs, increase to 100 over 2 minutes, sustain for 5 minutes, ramp down. Key metrics: p95 response time (<500ms), error rate (<1%), throughput (requests/sec). Test against staging with production-like data. Run from multiple geographic regions.

API response time optimization: Pagination (limit 20-50 items per page), field selection (GraphQL or sparse fieldsets), compression (gzip/Brotli reduces JSON payload by 70-80%), ETags for conditional requests (304 Not Modified saves bandwidth). Async processing for operations >500ms — return 202 Accepted with a polling endpoint or webhook callback.

Frontend performance budget: Total page weight under 1.5 MB (ideal) or 3 MB (max). JavaScript budget: under 300 KB compressed. CSS: under 100 KB. Fonts: under 100 KB (subset to needed characters, use font-display:swap). Track with Lighthouse CI in PR pipeline — fail builds that exceed budgets. Every 100 KB of JS adds ~350ms parse time on mid-range mobile.

Database connection management: PostgreSQL default max_connections=100 (each uses ~10 MB RAM). For 50 application servers with pool_size=10, use PgBouncer in transaction mode to multiplex 500 app connections onto 50 backend connections. Monitor with pg_stat_activity. Set statement_timeout=30s to prevent runaway queries from exhausting connections.

Async I/O performance: Python asyncio handles 10,000+ concurrent connections on a single thread vs 100-200 with synchronous threading. FastAPI with uvicorn processes 15,000-30,000 requests/second for I/O-bound workloads. Node.js event loop handles 50,000+ concurrent connections. Key: never block the event loop — offload CPU-intensive work to thread pools or separate processes.
